summary: The Communist state (1949-1990) formally known as the German Democratic Republic (GDR/DDR), governed throughout its existence by the Socialist Unity Party (SED) under leaders Walter Ulbricht, Erich Honecker, and finally Egon Krenz. Defined by the Berlin Wall it built in 1961 and the pervasive surveillance of the Stasi secret police (headed by spymaster Markus Wolf), it crushed the Uprising of 1953 with Soviet tanks, developed a distinctive "ostalgie"-remembered consumer culture around products like the Trabant car and Vita Cola, and was formally recognized as sovereign by West German Chancellor Willy Brandt's Ostpolitik before collapsing in the 1989 Peaceful Revolution and reunifying with West Germany.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How East Germany is identified in giveaways: the Communist state (1949-1990), formally the German Democratic Republic (GDR/DDR), that built the Berlin Wall, monitored its citizens with the Stasi secret police, and was reunified with its capitalist western neighbor, West Germany, after the Cold War.

clues.clue: Reunified with its western neighbor, West Germany, after the Cold War
examples: name this Communist country reunified with its western neighbor after the Cold War.

works.name: Government, the SED, and Walter Ulbricht's Leadership
works.description: East Germany was ruled throughout its existence by the Socialist Unity Party (SED), first under Walter Ulbricht (First Secretary, 1950-1971), then Erich Honecker (1971-1989), and finally Egon Krenz. Ulbricht's 1963 New Economic System replaced the initial Five-Year Plans and pursued a strategy of "overtaking without catching up" (formalized as the 1968 ESS policy). The country signed the Treaty of Zgorzelec with Poland, recognizing the Oder-Neisse line as its eastern border, and in 1972 its legislature passed its first-ever non-unanimous resolution, legalizing abortion.

clues.clue: Ruled throughout its existence by the Socialist Unity Party (SED), led first by Walter Ulbricht, then Erich Honecker, then finally Egon Krenz as its last leader
examples: The SED party led this country for its existence and its last leader, Egon Krenz, resigned after the destruction of a barrier built by Walter Ulbricht.
Walter Ulbricht led this former country, in which role he was succeeded by Erich Honecker and Egon Krenz, its last leader in 1989.

clues.clue: Ulbricht's 1963 New Economic System replaced the initial Five-Year Plans, pursuing a strategy of "overtaking without catching up" formalized by 1968's ESS policy
examples: This leader's New Economic System replaced the initial system of Five-Year Plans in East Germany.
Name this country where the strategy of "overtaking without catching up" was implemented by 1968's ESS policy.

clues.clue: Signed the Treaty of Zgorzelec with Poland, recognizing the Oder-Neisse line as its eastern border
examples: This nation signed the Treaty of Zgorzelec with an Eastern neighbor.

clues.clue: Its legislature passed its first-ever non-unanimous resolution in a 1972 vote legalizing abortion
examples: This country's legislature passed its first ever non-unanimous resolution in a 1972 vote legalizing abortion.

clues.clue: The term "Republikflucht" ("flight from the republic") described widespread emigration from the country in its early history

works.name: The Berlin Wall & Division from West Germany
works.description: In 1961 East Germany built the Berlin Wall (officially termed the "Anti-Fascist Protective Wall") to seal off West Berlin, the culmination of its physical division from West Germany. It later loosened border restrictions somewhat by signing the Transit Agreement, and then the Basic Treaty, with its western neighbor.

clues.clue: Built the Berlin Wall (the "Anti-Fascist Protective Wall") in 1961, sealing off West Berlin from the rest of the divided capital
examples: name this country that erected the Berlin Wall.
It policed its citizen with the Stasi, and separated itself from its capitalist western neighbor by building an "Anti-Fascist Protective Wall."

clues.clue: Signed the Transit Agreement, then the Basic Treaty, with West Germany, loosening border restrictions between the two countries
examples: This country signed the Transit Agreement with a neighboring country loosening border restrictions shortly before signing the Basic Treaty with that neighbor.

works.name: The Uprising of 1953
works.description: East Germany's defining early crisis was the Uprising of 1953: a construction workers' strike against a 10% production-quota increase under the "New Course" program (which had replaced "Constructing Socialism") spread into nationwide protest, with workers marching onto Stalin Allee under slogans like "we don't want to be slaves anymore." Aided by the underground Christian organization Young Congregation and the Bitterfeld committee's nine demands, the revolt was ultimately crushed by Soviet tanks as the People's Police (VoPo) fired on civilians; Soviet security chief Lavrentiy Beria was stripped of power in its aftermath, and the United States sent food-aid "Eisenhower Packages" to affected citizens. June 17 was later commemorated as a public holiday, "Day of German Unity," in West Germany.

clues.clue: A construction workers' strike against a 10% production-quota increase under the "New Course" program (which replaced "Constructing Socialism") developed into the Uprising of 1953, as workers marched onto Stalin Allee
examples: A construction workers' strike against higher work quotas developed into this country's Uprising of 1953.
After this country announced the New Course program, replacing Constructing Socialism, and including a ten percent increase in production quotas, workers walked out onto Stalin Alley.

clues.clue: Protesters used slogans like "we don't want to be slaves anymore" and "down with the government, down with the police," aided by the underground Christian organization Young Congregation and the Bitterfeld committee's nine demands
examples: Slogans which became popular in this country included "we don't want to be slaves anymore" and "down with the government, down with the police." This country was home to the underground Christian organization Young Congregation, which helped instigate an event whose nine demands were issued by the Bitterfeld committee.

clues.clue: Soviet tanks were deployed to crush the 1953 uprising, and the People's Police (VoPo) fired on civilians
examples: A workers' uprising in this country on June 17, 1953 is now celebrated as a "day of unity," but was put down at the time when the VoPo fired on civilians.
In this country, where the play The Plebeians Rehearse the Uprising takes place, Soviet tanks were deployed to end a 1953 popular uprising.

clues.clue: The United States sent food-aid "Eisenhower Packages" (containing peas, flour, milk, and lard) to citizens during the crisis
examples: People in this country received boxes containing peas, flour, milk, and lard that were known as Eisenhower Packages.

clues.clue: June 17 was later commemorated as a public holiday, "Day of German Unity," in West Germany; the state paper Neues Deutschland's coverage is remembered for asking "What occurred in" the city where the uprising started
examples: A workers' uprising in this country on June 17, 1953 is now celebrated as a "day of unity."
the state paper, the Neues Deutschland, asked "What occurred in" the city where this event started. The date of this event became a public holiday in West Germany.

clues.clue: Protestors disparaged three leaders with the slogan "A goatee beard, a paunch and spectacles are not what the people want!"; some were sent to the "Yellow Misery" prison
examples: Protestors in this country disparaged three of its leaders with the slogan, "A goatee beard, a paunch and spectacles are not what the people want!" A protest in this country that sent several people to the "Yellow Misery" prison could have been averted had the protestors known the government had rescinded its increased work quotas at VEBs.

examples: Beria was stripped of power following a strike of construction workers in this communist country, which erected a wall in its divided capital in 1961.

clues.clue: Gunter Grass's play The Plebeians Rehearse the Uprising is set during this event
examples: In this country, where the play The Plebeians Rehearse the Uprising takes place, Soviet tanks were deployed to end a 1953 popular uprising.

works.name: The Stasi & Surveillance State
works.description: The Ministry for State Security, known as the Stasi, employed as many as one in seven citizens as informants and was headed by legendary foreign-intelligence spymaster Markus Wolf, dubbed "the man without a face" for evading Western identification until 1978, who pioneered the use of seductive "Romeo" agents. The Stasi also oversaw a state-sponsored doping program at the SV Dynamo sports club, organized by SED member Manfred Ewald using drugs like Jenapharm's Oral-Turinabol under "State Plan 14.25." The state also replaced Catholic confirmation with a secular "youth consecration" (Jugendweihe) oath after the Central Committee weakened the clergy's influence.

clues.clue: The Stasi employed as many as one in seven citizens as informants
examples: Markus Wolf was an official in this country's secret police, which was known for employing as many as one in seven citizens as informants.

clues.clue: Foreign-intelligence spymaster Markus Wolf, dubbed "the man without a face" for evading Western identification, pioneered the use of "Romeo" agents to seduce and extract information from Western targets
examples: Before being identified by Swedish police, a spy from this country was only known as the "man without a face." That spymaster from this country, who pioneered using "Romeo" agents to extract information, was Markus Wolf.

clues.clue: The Stasi oversaw state-sponsored doping (organized by SED member Manfred Ewald at the SV Dynamo sports club, using drugs like Jenapharm's Oral-Turinabol under "State Plan 14.25"), which led former citizens to later launch legal action
examples: SED member Manfred Ewald organised state-sponsored doping in this country at the SV Dynamo sports club where it was often overseen by the Stasi.
Women who were formerly citizens of this country have launched legal actions over the use of drugs such as Jenapharm's Oral-Turinabol as part of its State Plan 14.25.

clues.clue: After the Central Committee weakened the clergy's influence, Catholic confirmation was replaced by a secular "youth consecration" (Jugendweihe) oath to the state, taken at age 14
examples: In this country, after the Central Committee weakened the clergy, Catholic confirmation was replaced with a "youth consecration" involving an oath to the state at age 14.

works.name: Ostalgie & Consumer/Daily Life
works.description: East German consumer culture is nostalgically remembered today as "ostalgie": the cheaply plastic-bodied, two-stroke Trabant car (nicknamed the "Trabi"), for which citizens waited years; Intershop stores, which accepted only the country's own "Forum checks" (introduced 1979) or foreign currency, alongside the Interhotel chain catering to visitors; the fabric Dederon (a form of Nylon-6), phased out during the 1970s oil crisis; a 1977 coffee crisis (sparked by a Brazilian "black frost") that led the state to invest in Vietnamese and Laotian coffee production; the fashion magazine Sibylle, founded in 1956, which offered weekly sewing tips; and the "little light man" (Ampelmännchen) pedestrian-signal figure in a straw hat, whose design was inspired by photographs of the country's leaders. Walter Ulbricht's 1958 "Ten Commandments" for a socialist "New Man" preceded a police crackdown on nudists practicing "free body culture" (FKK) at beaches, which vacationers nonetheless continued to visit by Trabant. The Coca-Cola knock-off Vita Cola, sought after by the protagonist of the film Goodbye, Lenin!, is also fondly remembered.

clues.clue: The Trabant, a cheaply-made plastic car with an obsolete two-stroke engine, was the country's signature automotive product, and citizens sometimes waited up to a decade to acquire one
examples: People in this country waited up to 10 years to acquire a loudly-backfiring plastic car called the Trabant.
It was the main manufacturing center for the Trabant, a piece of plastic used as a car.

clues.clue: Intershop stores accepted only the country's own "Forum checks" (introduced in 1979) or foreign currency, while the Interhotel chain catered to foreign visitors
examples: This country allowed only its own forum checks, as well as foreign currencies, at Intershop stores.
Chains like Interhotel and Intershop catered to visitors to this country, which introduced the Forum check as an alternative form of payment in 1979.

clues.clue: Companies phased out the fabric Dederon (a form of Nylon-6, used in mesh-net shopping bags) during the 1970s oil crisis
examples: During the 1970s oil crisis, companies in this country phased out the use of Dederon, a form of Nylon-6 used in mesh-net shopping bags.

clues.clue: A 1977 coffee crisis, sparked by a Brazilian "black frost" that wrecked that country's harvest, led the state to invest in Vietnamese and Laotian coffee production
examples: This country started to invest in Vietnamese and Laotian coffee production after a "black frost" that wrecked the Brazilian coffee harvest precipitated this country's 1977 coffee crisis.
This country attempted to solve its systemic coffee shortage by financing the Vietnamese coffee industry in the 1980s.

clues.clue: A photographer founded the fashion magazine Sibylle in 1956, which offered weekly sewing tips
examples: In 1956, a photographer in this country founded a fashion magazine which offered weekly sewing tips called Sibylle.

clues.clue: The "little light man" (Ampelmännchen), a walking figure in a straw hat on pedestrian signals, was designed based on photographs of the country's leaders
examples: A walking figure wearing a straw hat known as the "little light man" first appeared on this country's pedestrian signals.
A photo of another of this country's leaders in a straw hat inspired the design of its pedestrian signals.

examples: After one of its leaders proclaimed "Ten Commandments" for a type of "New Man" in 1958, police cracked down on nudists practicing "free body culture" on its beaches.
Vacationers in this country often drove to nude beaches in their Trabants.

clues.clue: Many senior officials lived on an elliptical street named for Soviet poet Vladimir Mayakovsky
examples: Many senior officials in this country lived on an elliptical street named for Vladimir Mayakovsky.

clues.clue: The character Alex searches for the country's Coca-Cola knock-off, Vita Cola, in the film Goodbye, Lenin!
examples: Alex looks for this country's Coke knock-off, Vita Cola, in the film Goodbye Lenin!.

works.name: Youth Culture & Organizations Under the SED
works.description: East German children joined the Ernst Thalmann Pioneers before graduating to the Free German Youth (FDJ). Walter Ulbricht cautiously attempted to liberalize youth culture in the early 1960s, but lost party support after the 1965 "Beat Revolt," a Leipzig riot sparked by Erich Honecker's ban on most rock music (and by nearby Beatles concerts across the border) while Ulbricht was abroad. Youth also gathered at "blue masses," informal rock concerts held in churches, and were supplied with coveted denim/blue jeans by the state through brands like Shanty and Wisent.

clues.clue: Children joined the Ernst Thalmann Pioneers before graduating to the Free German Youth (FDJ)
examples: Many children in this country joined the Thalmann Pioneers before graduating to the FDJ.
Organizations of this type sponsored by the East German government include one named for Ernst Thalmann and the FDJ.

clues.clue: Beatles concerts across the border helped spark the 1965 Leipzig "Beat Revolt," a youth riot against Erich Honecker's ban on most rock music, undermining Walter Ulbricht's cautious early-1960s attempt to liberalize youth culture
examples: Concerts by the Beatles in a neighboring country precipitated a 1965 youth-led "Beat Revolt" against authorities in this country.
Name this country, where Walter Ulbricht cautiously attempted to liberalize his country's youth culture in the early 1960s.

clues.clue: Youth attended "blue masses," informal rock concerts held in churches
examples: A June 1953 uprising took place in this country, whose youth attended "blue masses," rock concerts held in churches.

clues.clue: In the 1970s, the state supplied youth with coveted denim/blue jeans through brands like Shanty and Wisent
examples: In the 1970s, East Germany supplied its youth with this coveted product through brands like Shanty and Wisent.

works.name: Foreign Relations: Isolation, Ostpolitik, and African Alliances
works.description: West Germany's Hallstein Doctrine sought to diplomatically isolate East Germany by threatening to sever relations with any country that recognized it, until Willy Brandt's Ostpolitik replaced the doctrine and formally recognized East Germany as a sovereign state in the Basic Treaty. East Germany's Free German Youth (FDJ) sent "Friendship Brigades" to train workers in several African nations, and Guinea's first-ever international treaty allowed East Germany to establish a trade mission in Conakry. Historian Jeffrey Herf wrote about the country's "undeclared wars" against Israel, arguing that it distinguished between "moderate" and "extremist" Arab terrorists.

clues.clue: Diplomatically isolated by West Germany's Hallstein Doctrine, until Willy Brandt's Ostpolitik replaced it and formally recognized the country as sovereign in the Basic Treaty
examples: Isolating this country internationally was the goal of the Hallstein Doctrine, which was later replaced by Ostpolitik.

clues.clue: The Free German Youth (FDJ) sent "Friendship Brigades" to train workers in several African nations, and Guinea's first-ever international treaty allowed the country to establish a trade mission in Conakry
examples: This country's FDJ party sent Friendship Brigades to train workers in several African nations.
Guinea's first-ever international treaty allowed this country to establish a trade mission in Conakry, one of its several alliances with African countries.

clues.clue: Historian Jeffrey Herf wrote about the country's "undeclared wars" against Israel, arguing it distinguished between "moderate" and "extremist" Arab terrorists
examples: Jeffrey Herf claimed that this country distinguished between Arab "moderate" and "extremist" terrorists in a book about this country's "undeclared wars" against Israel.

works.name: The Peaceful Revolution & Fall (1989)
works.description: East Germany's collapse came via the 1989 Peaceful Revolution: Monday demonstrations centered at Leipzig's St. Nicholas Church, and the Alexanderplatz demonstrations in Berlin, rallied around the slogan "We are the people" (Wir sind das Volk); scholar Susanne Lohmann studied the role of "informational cascades" in the protest wave. Protesters cut the hammer-and-wheat-wreath emblem out of the national flag, hundreds of citizens sought refuge in a foreign embassy in Prague in the late 1980s, and the ruling SED party's collapse preceded the Berlin Wall's fall and the country's last leader, Egon Krenz, resigning.

examples: The slogan "We are the people" was at the center of the Monday demonstrations at the St. Nicholas Church in this country, as well as the 1989 Alexanderplatz demonstrations.

clues.clue: Protesters cut the hammer-and-wheat-wreath emblem out of the national flag during 1989 demonstrations against the ruling SED, which collapsed before the "2 Plus 4" Agreement and the Peaceful Revolution
examples: A hammer and a wreath of wheat was cut out of this country's flag during 1989 protests against its ruling Socialist Unity Party, which collapsed before the 2 Plus 4 Agreement and the Peaceful Revolution.

clues.clue: Hundreds of the country's citizens took refuge in a foreign embassy in Prague in the late 1980s
examples: In the late 1980s, a different country's embassy in Prague became home to hundreds of refugees from this country.

clues.clue: Political scientist Susanne Lohmann studied the role of "informational cascades" in the 1989 wave of protests
examples: Susanne Lohmann studied the role of informational cascades in a series of protests in this country.

clues.clue: The country's last leader, Egon Krenz, resigned after the fall of the Berlin Wall, the barrier originally built under Walter Ulbricht
examples: The SED party led this country for its existence and its last leader, Egon Krenz, resigned after the destruction of a barrier built by Walter Ulbricht.

works.name: Cultural Depictions in Literature & Film
works.description: East Germany looms large in Cold War-era fiction. Its most famous chronicler was Christa Wolf, an SED member for forty years, whose novel What Remains depicts life under Stasi surveillance and whose Kassandra allegorizes her own life in the country through the mythical Trojan priestess who watches Eumelos manipulate Priam into launching the Trojan War; revelations of Wolf's own cooperation with the Stasi later inflamed a public "Literaturstreit" (literature debate). Jenny Erpenbeck's 2021 novel Kairos depicts a teenager's affair with a married, middle-aged writer as the country collapses, while John le Carré's Cold War spy novels feature East Germany prominently, including The Spy Who Came in from the Cold, in which protagonist Alec Leamas is shot by its secret police. The Disney film Night Crossing fictionalized a real family's escape from the country via improvised hot-air balloon.

clues.clue: Christa Wolf, an SED member for forty years and one of the country's best-known writers, depicted life under Stasi surveillance in her novel What Remains; a public "Literaturstreit" was later inflamed by revelations of her own cooperation with the Stasi
examples: Wolf was one of the best-known writers of this country, where she was a member of the Socialist Unity Party for forty years. Her novel What Remains describes life under surveillance by the Stasi in this country.
The literature debate, or Literaturstreit, was inflamed by the revelation that Wolf had cooperated with the Stasi.

clues.clue: While living in the country, Christa Wolf wrote the novel Kassandra, in which the cursed narrator watches Eumelos manipulate Priam into launching the Trojan War, largely as an allegory for life in East Germany
examples: While living in this country, an author wrote a novel whose cursed narrator watches Eumelos manipulate Priam into launching the Trojan War.

clues.clue: A teenager dates a married, middle-aged writer as the country collapses in Jenny Erpenbeck's 2021 novel Kairos
examples: A teenager dates a married, middle-aged writer as this country collapses in the 2021 novel Kairos.

clues.clue: Protagonist Alec Leamas is shot by this country's secret police in John le Carré's The Spy Who Came In From The Cold
examples: Alec Leamas is shot by what Soviet satellite's secret police in The Spy Who Came In From The Cold?

clues.clue: A British agent pretends to defect to this country as part of a "Circus" operation run by George Smiley in a le Carré spy novel
examples: In a spy novel, a British agent pretends to defect to this country as part of a "Circus" operation run by George Smiley.

clues.clue: A 1963 novel (which Willem Frederik Hermans considered plagiarized) featured one of the country's tribunals mistakenly identifying a Jewish security chief as a traitor
examples: In a 1963 novel that Willem Frederik Hermans held to be plagiarized, one of this country's tribunals mistakenly identifies a Jewish security chief as a traitor.

clues.clue: The Disney film Night Crossing fictionalized a real family's escape from the country via improvised hot-air balloon
examples: The Disney film Night Crossing fictionalized a family's escape from this country in an improvised hot-air balloon.

comprehensive_summary: East Germany, formally the German Democratic Republic (GDR/DDR), was the Communist state that existed from 1949 to 1990, ruled throughout by the Socialist Unity Party (SED) under Walter Ulbricht (First Secretary, 1950-1971), then Erich Honecker (1971-1989), and finally Egon Krenz. Ulbricht's 1963 New Economic System replaced the initial Five-Year Plans and pursued a strategy of "overtaking without catching up"; the country signed the Treaty of Zgorzelec with Poland recognizing the Oder-Neisse line, and in 1972 became the first to pass a non-unanimous legislative resolution, legalizing abortion. In 1961 it built the Berlin Wall (the "Anti-Fascist Protective Wall"), later loosening border restrictions via the Transit Agreement and Basic Treaty with West Germany. Its defining early crisis was the Uprising of 1953: a construction workers' strike against a 10% quota increase under the "New Course" program spread nationwide, with slogans like "we don't want to be slaves anymore" and support from the underground Young Congregation and the Bitterfeld committee's nine demands, before Soviet tanks and the People's Police (VoPo) crushed it — an event later commemorated as "Day of German Unity" in West Germany, and after which Soviet security chief Lavrentiy Beria was stripped of power. The country was defined by pervasive surveillance: the Stasi employed as many as one in seven citizens as informants, was headed by spymaster Markus Wolf ("the man without a face," pioneer of seductive "Romeo" agents), organized state-sponsored athletic doping under Manfred Ewald at SV Dynamo using drugs like Oral-Turinabol, and oversaw the replacement of Catholic confirmation with a secular "youth consecration" oath. Its consumer culture is remembered today as "ostalgie": the plastic-bodied Trabant car, Intershop stores accepting only "Forum checks," the fabric Dederon, a 1977 coffee crisis addressed by investing in Vietnamese and Laotian coffee, the fashion magazine Sibylle, the straw-hatted "little light man" pedestrian signal, Ulbricht's 1958 "Ten Commandments" for a socialist "New Man" alongside a crackdown on nudist beach culture, and the Vita Cola soft drink. Its youth joined the Ernst Thalmann Pioneers and then the Free German Youth (FDJ); Ulbricht's cautious early-1960s liberalization of youth culture collapsed after the 1965 Leipzig "Beat Revolt" against Honecker's rock-music ban. Diplomatically isolated by West Germany's Hallstein Doctrine until Willy Brandt's Ostpolitik secured its recognition via the Basic Treaty, East Germany also cultivated African alliances, sending FDJ "Friendship Brigades" abroad and opening a trade mission in Guinea, while historian Jeffrey Herf documented its "undeclared wars" against Israel. The state finally collapsed in the 1989 Peaceful Revolution, driven by Leipzig's Monday demonstrations and the Alexanderplatz protests under the banner "We are the people," the flight of citizens to a Prague embassy, and the SED's collapse, culminating in the fall of the Berlin Wall and Egon Krenz's resignation. The country also loomed large in Cold War fiction: Christa Wolf's What Remains and Kassandra (a Trojan War allegory for East German life), Jenny Erpenbeck's Kairos, John le Carré's Cold War spy novels (including The Spy Who Came In From The Cold), and the Disney film Night Crossing, about a family's hot-air-balloon escape.
recursive_suggestions: Berlin Wall — has independent tossup/bonus presence (T17) beyond its role in East German history; distinct topic covering its art, Checkpoint Charlie, and Peter Fechter's death
German reunification — independent answerline (T20, T23) with its own Two Plus Four Treaty/Article 23 clue set; excluded here except where framed as East Germany's collapse
West Germany — independent answerline (T8, T9) covering the Berlin Airlift, occupation sectors, and Bonn-era politics; excluded here except where directly juxtaposed with East Germany
Willy Brandt — recurring figure (Ostpolitik, Basic Treaty, Guillaume affair); candidate for own topic given independent bonus-part presence

Walter Ulbricht / Erich Honecker — East Germany's two major leaders each have substantial independent material (Ulbricht's New Economic System and Berlin Wall order; Honecker's 1971-1989 rule) that could support standalone Figure topics
Markus Wolf — recurring spymaster with independent bonus-part presence (B1P3); candidate for his own topic
Christa Wolf — recurring author with independent bonus-part presence (B2P1-3, B8P1); already likely has (or warrants) her own Literature topic, watch for overlap
Neue Ostpolitik — independent answerline (T16) for Brandt's broader Eastern policy; excluded here except where directly tied to East German recognition
Hallstein Doctrine — recurring but has independent bonus-part presence (B14P3); could become its own topic
1953 Uprising in East Germany — treated here as a section, but has enough independent clue density (B11P3 and others) to become a standalone event topic if it recurs further
